title: Chapter 14: Tears of the Destroyer
paragraphs: The world was fading slowly, like an ancient elder resigned to the inevitable. Autumn in the wastelands beyond the Great Road smelled of dry wormwood, rust, and dust. Yashil walked through the thickets of dead shrubs, leaving no tracks. Over the last few moons, he had tried three times to approach travelers to offer help. Each time the response was thrown stones, abuse, and the ring of bared steel.

One dwarven merchant lashed him with a whip just because Yashil picked up a leather pouch of tobacco he had dropped and tried to return it. The scar on the goblin's shoulder still pulled unpleasantly, but there was no resentment in his heart. There was only a quiet, lingering sadness. He understood that centuries of hatred could not be erased with one kind gesture. It would take time.

The sun was setting, painting the horizon in the colors of clotted blood and old copper. The wind died down, giving way to a ringing, almost glass-like silence. And suddenly this silence was broken by a sound that completely did not fit into the landscape of the wastelands.
It was not the roar of a predator or the clank of armor. It was a heavy, stifled sob.
Yashil silently parted the dry branches of the thorn bush and froze.

On a small clearing surrounded by twisted, dead trees, sat a creature out of nightmares. A giant demon of the Legion. His body, woven of black, oily resin and bone, towered even in a sitting position. Along his massive arms and back pulsed crimson veins, emitting a faint smell of sulfur and heated ash. Huge curved horns crowned his head, and the claws on his paws looked like curved elven blades, designed to rip open fortress gates.

For any living creature of this world, meeting a demon meant a torturous death. They were pure, concentrated destruction, a blind instrument of extermination. Yashil's instinct, forged by generations of goblins, screamed at him to press himself into the ground, merge with the dirt, and not breathe.
But the goblin was not looking at the horns or the claws. He was looking at what the demon held in its monstrous palms.

There, like a tiny gray ball, lay a field lark with a broken wing. The demon leaned over it, and his movements were incredibly, painfully clumsy. He was trying to warm the bird. Magic flowed from under his black claws—he was desperately trying to heal the broken bone.

But the magic of the Abyss did not know how to create. The dark energy, touching the fragile little body, instead of mending the wound, caused the bird's feathers to turn black and crumble into dust. The lark twitched one last time and went still.

The demon froze. His shoulders, capable of withstanding the blow of a battering ram, dropped heavily. He let out a low, rumbling sound, full of such unbearable, soul-ripping longing that it took Yashil's breath away. The giant covered his face with his hands, and through his claws a heavy, scalding drop fell to the dry earth.
The demon was crying.

At that moment, the universe for Yashil made a quiet but irreversible turn. The illusions that the High Peoples had built for centuries, dividing the world into "pure" and "monsters," crumbled into dust.
The goblin did not hide. He parted the branches and took a step onto the clearing. A dry twig snapped under his bare foot.

The sound rang like a gunshot. The demon reacted with frightening speed. He lunged to his feet, spinning around to the source of the sound. Crimson veins flared with an aggressive light, and his metallic claws lengthened. Two ruby eyes stared at Yashil from under black brow ridges. In his posture read an instinctive readiness to tear apart anyone who dared to see his weakness.
But Yashil did not run. He did not raise his hands in a plea for mercy. He stood straight, looking up at the four-meter-tall mountain of black death, his green eyes radiating absolute, serene calm.
"You are crying over a bird," Yashil said quietly, not as a question but stating a fact.
The demon froze. He expected panic, a screech, an attempt to escape. But this tiny goblin, who barely reached his knee, looked at him without a trace of fear.

"Go away, green-skin," the demon growled. His voice sounded like the grinding of stones in a tectonic fault. "My nature is to kill. I am destruction. Run before my darkness decides that you are suitable prey."
"Your darkness killed the lark," Yashil replied just as calmly. "But your tears say that you did not want this. A weapon does not mourn those it strikes."

The demon exhaled noisily, in bursts. The fire in his eyes dimmed, replaced by confusion. He slowly lowered his paws, and his claws retracted. The giant, as if all strength had been drained from him, sank onto the fallen trunk of a tree, which groaned piteously under his weight.

"My name is Kirah," he said hollowly, without raising his head. "I was forged in the forges of the Legion. From day one, one truth was beaten into me: pity is a defect. Compassion is a disease. I destroyed dwarven settlements. I broke elven shields. But every time I heard the cries of those who lost their loved ones, something inside me broke. I looked at my hands and hated them. Hated myself."
Kirah looked at the dead lark at his feet.

"I deserted," the demon continued. "I went into the wastelands. I thought that if I could save at least one tiny life... if I could heal even the broken wing of a bird... I would prove to the universe that I am not just a monster. That I have the right to exist for something other than carrying death. But my magic... it is saturated with necrosis. Everything I touch, wishing to warm—burns."
He raised his eyes to Yashil, full of hopeless, dark agony.
"Your people are despised, goblin. But you at least were not created as absolute evil. You can choose. But I am a curse in its pure form. There is no place for me in this world."
The wind rustled through the dry grass.
Yashil slowly approached the giant demon. He stopped a step from his massive knees, looked at Kirah's scarred black hands, and then calmly sat down beside him right on the cold ground.

"If you were absolute evil, Kirah, you would not suffer from not knowing how to heal," Yashil's voice was soft, like water washing over sharp stones. "Evil does not seek excuses and does not seek redemption. It simply consumes. The fact that you are pained by your nature—makes you alive."
"But I cannot create anything!" Kirah exhaled in despair. "I can only break!"

"Then break what prevents others from living," Yashil said simply. "If your hands were created to crush walls, then one day you will meet walls that need to be crushed to set someone free. It is not magic that makes us who we are. It is what we use it for."

Kirah froze. In all his life, filled with orders, pain, and the clank of steel, no one had ever spoken to him like this. No one had seen him as anything other than a tool. And now, at the edge of a fading world, a tiny green-skinned outcast sat beside him and spoke words to him that healed better than any magic of light.
The demon carefully, afraid to cause harm, reached out a huge finger and touched Yashil's tiny shoulder. The goblin did not flinch.
"You are very strange," Kirah whispered with a rumbling voice. "You must have been told that demons cannot be trusted."

"I have been told a lot of things," Yashil smiled faintly, looking at the fading sky. "They said my mother died because she was weak. They said goblins were dirt under the feet of the High Elves. If you believe everything said in this world, then it's better not to be born at all. I prefer to believe what I see. And I see a being with a huge heart."

That night, the two outcasts—the goblin who refused to hate, and the demon who refused to destroy—sat by the fire until dawn. They were silent, but in that silence there was more meaning than in all the high-flown treatises of the White Hall.
The bond born between them was fragile, like the wing of that lark, but it was destined to one day change the fates of entire nations.
